summary: Cathy O'Neil presents the classic blue-eyed/green-eyed island puzzle, where islanders must leave if they discover their own eye color. A visitor's seemingly redundant statement that 'some people have green eyes' triggers a logical chain reaction that eventually forces all green-eyed people to leave after N days, where N is their number. The puzzle's conundrum is that the visitor added no new factual information—everyone already knew green-eyed people existed—yet his statement transforms the situation entirely. O'Neil explores whether this represents an unstable equilibrium or demonstrates how 'common knowledge' differs from individual knowledge.
key_points: The puzzle demonstrates inductive reasoning: if you see N green-eyed people and they don't leave after N-1 days, you must also have green eyes
The visitor's statement seems information-free when many green-eyed people exist, since everyone already knows some people have green eyes
The key insight is that the statement creates 'common knowledge'—not just that green eyes exist, but that everyone knows everyone knows this
The puzzle raises questions about equilibrium: was the original social silence a stable or unstable state?
Aaron and Jamie coined the phrase 'a green-eyed/blue-eyed thing' for apparently information-free facts that change everything
money_quotes: By social convention they never discuss eye color, because there's a tragic rule that states that, if you ever figure out your eye color, you have to leave the island within 24 hours.
The guy who started this whole mess really didn't do much. He just stated what was obvious to everyone already on the island.
So really the guy just started a count-down more than anything.
Is it not an equilibrium at all? Or is it an unstable equilibrium?
It's a green-eyed/ blue-eyed thing, which means it's an apparently information-free fact which changes everything.
key_insight: Sometimes stating what everyone already knows individually creates common knowledge that transforms a stable situation into an inevitable cascade—information can be about shared awareness, not just facts.
